A Foundation for Differentiable Logics using
Dependent Type Theory
Reynald Affeldt1*†, Alessandro Bruni2†, Ekaterina
Komendantskaya3,4†, Natalia ´Slusarz4†, Kathrin Stark4†
1National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology
(AIST), Japan.
2IT-University of Copenhagen, Denmark.
3Southampton University, UK.
4Heriot-Watt University, UK.
*Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): reynald.affeldt@aist.go.jp;
Contributing authors: brun@itu.dk; e.komendantskaya@soton.ac.uk;
nds1@hw.ac.uk; k.stark@hw.ac.uk;
†Author Contribution: All authors contributed equally to this work.
Abstract
Differentiable logics are a family of quantitative logics originated in the machine
learning literature. Because of their origin, differentiable logics often come
equipped with analytic properties that guarantee that they are differentiable.
However, they usually lack an accompanying theory that describes their algebraic
and proof-theoretic properties. Meanwhile, fuzzy logics, seen as substructural
logics, have been studied algebraically and proof-theoretically, and some fuzzy
logics with desirable analytic properties have also been used in machine learning.
Our aim is to systematically compare analytic, algebraic and proof-theoretical
properties of both fuzzy and differentiable logics.
To this end, we formalize differentiable and fuzzy logics in a unified framework,
encoded using the MathComp library in the Rocq proof assistant. We propose a
single language specification to encompass multiple logics, using intrinsic typing
to only allow valid and well-typed formulas for each of the logics that we encode:
Yager, Lukasiewicz, G¨odel and product fuzzy logics, as well as the differentiable
logics DL2 and STL. Algebraically, we show how these logics can be interpreted
using residuated lattices, which are prevalent in the theory of substructural logics. Analytically, we formalise the existence of a positive derivative for certain
logical connectives, and to this end we formalise L’Hˆopital’s, contributing it to

the MathComp library. Proof-theoretically, we formalise established sequent calculi for fuzzy logics, and we propose new sequent calculi for DL2 and STL∞, and
formalise their soundness in our framework.
Keywords: Machine Learning, Loss Functions, Differentiable Logics, Logic and
Semantics, Interactive Theorem Proving
Differences with the conference paper
Some of the results presented in this journal submission appeared in the paper [2], published at ITP’24. This journal submission extends considerably the results presented
at ITP’24.
We have revised the ITP’24 results and Rocq formalisation in two main ways.
Firstly, we have added a new characterisation of differentiable logics in terms of
residuated lattices, an algebraic structure that was traditionally used to give algebraic
semantics to fuzzy logics [29]. This characterisation was missing in the differentiable
logic/machine learning literature; our work brings semantic uniformity to the analysis
of fuzzy differentiable logics and differentiable logics proposed in machine learning
papers.
Secondly, while fuzzy logics received a thorough proof-theoretic treatment in the
literature [29, 39], new, machine-learning inspired differentiable logics had no prooftheoretic semantics. Here, we formulate a new sound sequent calculus for DL2 and a
sound sequent calculus for a restricted version of STL.
Specifically, the following sections and subsections are new:
• In Sect. 4.1, the syntax and semantics of differentiable logics is reformulated to
fit within a residuated lattice framework. In Sect. 6, we provide the corresponding
proofs.
• In Sect. 7.1, we proved hat the n-ary conjunction of STLν converges to min when
ν tends towards +∞and is therefore a lattice operation.
• In Sect. 7.4, we have generalised L’Hˆopital’s rule.
• In Sect. 8, we define hypersequent calculi for differentiable logics and prove their
soundness.
• In Sect. 9.2, we give an application of differentiable logics in machine learning.
1 Introduction
This work aims at contributing to the field of formal verification of machine learning
seen as the study of algorithms that learn statistically from data. Neural networks are
the most common technical device used in machine learning. The standard learning
algorithms (such as gradient descent) use a loss function L : Rm ×Rn →R to optimise
the neural network’s parameters (say, θ) to fit the input-output vectors given by the
data in a way that the loss L(x, y) is minimised. This optimisation objective is usually
denoted as minθ L(x, y).
2

Most approaches to verification of neural networks consist of an automated procedure based on SMT solving, abstract interpretation, or branch-and-bound techniques
(see, e.g., Albarghouthi’s survey [7]). Verification typically applies after training
because traditional learning is purely data-driven and thus agnostic to verification
properties. In contrast, property-guided training takes place once the verification properties are stated. More precisely, verification of neural networks consists of two parts:
statement and verification of a given property, and training of the neural network that
optimises the neural network’s parameters towards satisfying the given property.
However, naively or manually performed mapping of a logical property to an optimisation task results in major discrepancies (as shown by Casadio et al. [18]). This
suggests the need to have principled tools for property-guided training. One approach
is to provide programming language support for property-guided development of
neural networks that involves specification, verification, and optimisation in a safe-byconstruction environment. As an example, Vehicle [22,24] provides this support in the
form of a Haskell DSL, with (1) a higher-order typed specification language, in which
required neural network properties can be clearly documented, and (2) type-driven
compilation, which can take care of correct-by-construction translation of properties
into both the language of neural network verifiers it interfaces with and loss functions.
To generate loss functions from a logical property, one can use differentiable logics
(DLs). One possibility for such differentiable logics are the well-studied fuzzy logics
that date back to the works of Lukasiewicz and G¨odel [45].
Example 1.1. The semantics of a differentiable logic is a map J·K from a given
syntax to a real number-valued function. Consider, as an example of a fuzzy logic,
product logic [45, Sect. 3]. Let ϕ be a small fragment of propositional syntax: ϕ := a ∈
A | ⊥| φ1 ∧φ2, where φ1, φ2 ∈ϕ, and A is a set of real number-valued propositional
variables. In product logic, the semantics of this small syntax would be JaK := a,
J⊥K := 0, and Jφ1 ∧φ2K := Jφ1K × Jφ2K.
As an alternative, both verification and machine-learning communities formulated
differentiable logics such as DL2 [27] and STL [47].
Existing differentiable logics are very different from one another. For example,
they do not agree on the domains of the resulting loss functions: fuzzy logics have as
domain the closed interval1 [0, 1], the original domain of DL2 (reversed in this work
for the purpose of consistent treatment of greatest value as true) corresponds to the
Lawvere quantale [35] [0, +∞[, and STLν’s domain is ]−∞, +∞[ (all intervals begin
equipped with the usual ordering on reals). Each domain has a designated value or
interval for truth and falsity. In consequence, it is unclear which differentiable logic is
more suitable for property-guided training and how the differentiable logics compare.
Empirical evaluations, such as Flinkow et al. [28], cannot provide a clear principled
answer to the problem of developing suitable differentiable logics.
This situation calls for a principled unified mathematical study of differentiable
logics. One way to do this is to take into account a century-long tradition of algebraic
1We use the notation ]a, b[ for intervals open on both sides, [a, b] for closed, and [a, b[ and ]a, b] for closed
only on the left/right side respectively.
3

and proof-theoretic studies of fuzzy logics [29,39], and show how those well-established
results extend to, and shed the light on, the new differentiable logics proposed in the
machine learning context. On the other hand, machine learning comes with is own
approach to analysing mathematical (this time understood as analytical) properties
of differentiable logics. Thus, the second research question to ask is whether, and how,
the century-old fuzzy logics (rendered as differentiable logics) fit into this machine
learning context. This research program poses a challenge, due to both the large zoo
of existing differentiable logics as well as the diversity of the mathematical methods
used by different communities to motivate and analyse these logics.
Why reconciling algebraic and analytic traditions is hard for differentiable logics?
On the one hand, even though differentiable logics derived from fuzzy logics can be
characterised by the existing algebraic semantics of fuzzy logics [29,39], the majority of
these logics do not enjoy desirable analytic properties, such as smoothness or shadowlifting [47], which are key for machine learning applications. On the other hand, the
new differentiable logics inspired by machine learning [27,47] are not very well understood from the algebraic point of view. For example, Varnai and Dimarogonas [47]
prove that differentiable logic operations cannot satisfy simultaneously the properties
of shadow-lifting, idempotence, and associativity. Yet, associativity is traditionally the
key property in the algebraic analysis of differentiable logics [29].
Similarly, proof-theoretic study of differentiable logics is lacking. While some fuzzy
logics enjoy sound and/or complete propositional sequent calculi [39], the new differentiable logics suggested by machine learning communities have not been studied from
the proof-theoretic perspective.
We believe that a systematic investigation of differentiable logics is a necessary step
towards the development of a reliable neural network verification tool, and towards
formal verification of machine learning in general. We therefore propose to formalise
them in an interactive theorem prover, providing a unified formalisation of differentiable logics in which their properties can be compared rigorously and in which one can
verify the translation from properties to loss functions. For that purpose, we chose the
Rocq prover: firstly, because it has been used to carry out numerous formalisations of
logics and programming languages; secondly, because the Mathematical Components
project provides libraries to handle the algebraic properties of differentiable logics
(using MathComp [26]) and the analytic properties such as shadow-lifting (using
MathComp-Analysis [1]).
Our approach to a systematic investigation of differentiable logics is to build on
top of previous work that has already proposed a common presentation of differentiable logics [49] and to advance the understanding of mathematical properties of
differentiable logics along the following three axes:
• Algebraic: The idea is to define algebraic semantics for differentiable logics that
guide our general understanding and comparison of their properties. For that purpose, we use residuated lattices. Residuated lattices are algebraic structures that
have been suggested as a suitable model for quantitative substructural logics [29,39].
We show which of the new differentiable logics can be mapped to residuated lattices and make a comparative inventory of the structural properties of the logical
connectives of differentiable logics.
4

• Analytic: This axis concerns the properties of differentiable logics that rely on the
use of real analysis, because of the use of limits and differentiation. A central example is shadow-lifting already mentioned above. It states that the resulting functions
should have partial derivatives that can characterise the idea of gradual improvement in training [47]. For example, the translation of a conjunction should evaluate
to a higher value if the value of one of its conjuncts increases. Example 1.1 illustrates how such a conjunction may look. We formally prove or disprove (the latter
on pen and paper) shadow-lifting, for each chosen differentiable logic.
• Proof-theoretic: Proof theory, and in particular Gentzen sequent calculi, have
long been used to study computational properties of logics. While fuzzy logics
already have well-defined proof-theoretic semantics [39], the question of whether
new differentiable logics may enjoy such semantics is still open. We present sound
sequent calculi for DL2 [27] and STL∞, and argue why STLν is unlikely to have a
Gentzen-style proof calculus (see Sect. 4.1.2 for definitions of STL∞and STLν).
The approach explained above led us to the following contributions:
1. We explain how to encode known differentiable logics in a single generic syntax using
Rocq, building on known techniques for logic embedding, such as taking advantage
of dependent types to achieve intrinsic typing. The formalisation is comprehensive
and extensible for future use.
2. We demonstrate how to use the Mathematical Components libraries for our purpose. For example, we take advantage of algebraic tactics [44] to automate algebraic
and soundness proofs, and we develop reusable lemmas, such as L’Hˆopital’s rule to
establish shadow-lifting.
3. As a result of our formalisation, we are able to find and fix errors in the literature,
and to produce original results missing parts of the shadow-lifting proofs, and
soundness of DL2 and STL∞with respect to their new sequent calculi.
The paper proceeds as follows. Section 2 gives a preliminary introduction to differentiable logics, including their original definitions and initial broad comparison.
Section 3 provides mathematical background on residuated lattices and their axioms.
Section 4 gives an overview of the main results of the paper. Section 5 explains how
one can define differentiable logics in Rocq using a generic encoding, including a
translation function producing the semantics. Section 6 focuses on the formalisation
of algebraic properties
for differentiable logics. In Sect. 7, we demonstrate the formal verification of properties of differentiable logics relying on real analysis. In Sect. 8, we show the formalisation
of soundness with respect to hypersequent calculi of fuzzy differentiable logics and
define a new calculus for DL2. Section 9 explains the application of differentiable logics
in property-guided training by means of an example, and shows the formalisation of
an example property. We discuss related work in Sect. 10 and conclude in Sect. 11.

Fuzzy differentiable logics are the most well-researched in terms of their algebraic
properties, due to the long history of logic research. In particular, while not presented
this way by Krieken et al. [45], who first suggest their application as differentiable
logics, they are traditionally defined using residuated lattices [29]—the definition followed in Definition 2.1—which we explain in more detail in Sect. 3. Additionally, they
are the only differentiable logics to come with a proof theoretic interpretation.
The remaining differentiable logics have their origin in machine learning.
DL2 is defined around the concept of incorporating comparisons between terms,
often present in machine learning properties (e.g., robustness), as the core part of the
language [27]:

This initial introduction allows us to point out the main differences in the treatment of differentiable logics as well as compare them in terms of the properties they
satisfy in their original state. We have already noted that fuzzy differentiable logics
have better investigated properties, particularly algebraic, as they can be defined using
the well-known structure of residuated lattices. In contrast DL2 introduces novel syntactic elements tailored to the application in neural network training (see Sect. 9 for
a detailed explanation of the training process).
Additionally, from the perspective of their application, only DL2 and STL, the
logics with their roots in machine learning are differentiable, as fuzzy differentiable
logics include connectives interpreted as min and max. This can make them more
convenient in usage for training, where differentiability is a desired property.
3 Mathematical background on residuated lattices
In this section, we recall the notion of residuated lattice that will be key to organise
our formalisation of differentiable logics.
Residuated lattices have been proposed as a suitable algebraic structure to characterise a range of substructural logics [29], i.e., logics that are stronger than
intuitionistic logic, but weaker than classical logic. These include linear logic, paraconsistent and relevant logics, and, most importantly for our current study, real
number-valued and fuzzy logics [29,39]. We devote this section to definition of residuated lattices, and will use the knowledge of these algebraic properties as a guiding
principle in later sections.
In the below definition we use real numbers as a carrier set, since real numbers are
of specific interest in this work. Often, the below definition is given with an abstract
carrier set.
Definition 3.1. An algebra RL = (R, ∨, ∧, ⊙, ⇒, 1) is called a residuated lattice if
• (R, ∨, ∧) is a lattice (i.e., ∨, ∧are commutative, associative, and mutually absorptive, and thus give rise to a lattice order ≤),
• (R, ⊙, 1) is a monoid (i.e., ⊙is commutative and associative with unit element 1),
• x ⊙y ≤z iff y ≤x ⇒z for all x, y, z ∈R.
We say that a residuated lattice is distributive if x ∧(y ∨z) ≤(x ∧y) ∨(x ∧z) for
all x, y, z ∈R.
The above definition gives rise to the axioms R1–R10 of Table 2.
To see why this algebra has played a pivotal role in substructural logics, notice that
the lattice structure (R, ∨, ∧) corresponds to the additive fragment of linear logic [6].
The monoidal structure (R, ⊙, 1) corresponds to the multiplicative fragment of linear
logic; for example, ⊙can be given by multiplication on real numbers. See Galatos et
al. [29] for a more detailed analysis of linear logic interpretation in residuated lattices.
Finally, thanks to the “residuation axiom” x⊙y ≤z iff y ≤x ⇒z (R10 in Table 2),
we can interpret implication as an adjoint operator to ⊙, which gives a quantitative
interpretation of implication in residuated lattices.

4 Overview of the approach and main results
We first recall the syntax and semantics of differentiable logics using one generic syntax
(generic enough to represent all differentiable logics as well as concrete applications)
and an informal, yet complete explanation of their semantics (Sect. 4.1). We split one
of the differentiable logics, STL, into two versions: STLν and STL∞, based on the
value of parameter ν used which is used in its semantics. We then explain our approach
which consists of three axes (algebraic, analytic, proof-theoretic, see Sect. 1) for the
properties we wish to establish across all differentiable logics. We then summarise the
known properties of different differentiable logics as well as an overview of our results
which we formalise throughout this work (Sect. 4.3).
4.1 A unified presentation of differentiable logics
´Slusarz et al. [49] suggest a common syntax for multiple differentiable logics, calling it
the logic of differentiable logics, and subsequently obtain different differentiable logics
via different interpretation functions. In the following, we summarise the syntactic
and semantic features of differentiable logics following this formulation. Compared to
´Slusarz et al. [49] and the previous version of this paper [2], we extend this syntax to
cohere with the set of connectives used in other studies of substructural logics [29,39].
4.1.1 Syntax of differentiable logics
The syntax of differentiable logics consists of types and expressions (Fig. 1). It includes
a mixture of features present in the individual differentiable logics, as outlined in
Sect. 2.
Types are given by Boolean values or real numbers, indices, vectors, and function
types (Fun m n for unary functions and Fun2 l m n for binary functions). The addition
of the binary functions, absent in the previous version of this work [2], was deemed
necessary when considering real-life properties, which can often include functions with
two arguments. Real expressions are either real numbers (ranged over by r) or the
result of addressing a vector of real numbers. Vectors (ranged over by v) can be
addressed using indices (ranged over by i) or can be obtained by applying unary
or binary functions (ranged over by f). Formulas (or Boolean expressions, ranged
over by p) are built from ⊤and ⊥, from application of the predicates ≤and = to
10

real expressions, or by combining formulas using logical connectives (¬, ⊙, ⊕, ⇒,
∧, and ∨). Here, we combine the best features of the original syntax of both DL2
(Definition 2.2), with its comparisons and fuzzy differentiable logics (Definition 2.1),
which follow residuated lattices.
Because STLν by Varnai and Dimarogonas [47] lacks associativity for ∨and ∧
(Definition 2.3), we define them as n-ary connectives separately using the following

We forgo the originally included quantifiers, lambda and let expressions [49] to
obtain a simpler core language in which the three types of properties of interest
(algebraic, analytic, and proof-theoretic) can be studied.
4.1.2 Semantics of differentiable logics
To define a differentiable logic, one defines an interpretation function J·KDL that,
given an expression in the syntax of differentiable logics, returns a function on real
numbers. We introduce all differentiable logics in a generic way and use the meta notation J·KDL to refer to a range of interpretation functions, with differentiable logic ∈
{G¨odel, Lukasiewicz, Yagerr, product, DL2, STLν, STL∞}. Some logics—Yagerr and
STLν—are parametrised by a non-negative r, ν ∈R which are logics with a different
fixed ν each. Note also the existence of STL∞—a particular case of STLν where ν is
∞; its relevance and behaviour is discussed in detail in Sect. 4.
Table 5 shows the interpretation of all differentiable logics. First are the three
differentiable logics based on well-known fuzzy logics: G¨odel, Lukasiewicz [48], and
product [45]. We also include a fuzzy logic based on Yagerr t-norms (binary functions
which are commutative, associative, monotonic and have an identity element), which
we refer to as Yagerr logic from now on). Yagerr t-norms belong to a wider family
of parametrised t-norms [32] the behaviour of which changes depending on the value
of the parameter. We assume r > 0 which makes the Yagerr logic isomorphic to
 Lukasiewicz (and equal to Lukasiewicz at r = 1) and which at r tending to ∞converges
to G¨odel’s connectives.
All fuzzy logics have the interpretation domain of [0, 1] ⊂R. Other logics have
different domains: DL2 [27] has the interpretation domain ]−∞, 0], and STLν [47] the
domain ]−∞, +∞[.
Note the lack of semantics of ⊤and ⊥for STLν and DL2. Both DLs define the
notion of truth and falsity using intervals ]0, ∞[ and ]−∞, 0[ for STLν respectively,
and ]−∞, 0[ for false for DL2.
Also note that while negation is undefined for DL2 as a structural transformation
of the syntax of formulas, it is implemented at the level of atomic comparison only,
and negation on composite formulas is provided as syntactic sugar [27, Sect. 3]. For
example, consider J3 = 3KDL2 = 0. In DL2, it is not defined in term of J3 = 3KDL2 = 0,
but DL2 provides an interpretation for the symbol ̸= separately (we do not include
all comparisons within the syntax).
11

The binary predicates ≤and = are defined in a way that ensures that they are
interpreted within the chosen real interval for the given differentiable logic. Note that
the interpretation of ≤does not necessarily cohere with the interpretation of ⇒as
might be expected. This has been done to follow more closely the behaviour of the
interpretation function for the comparisons for DL2 (with additional allowances made
for the domain)—that is, emphasising more the standard way in which real numbers
can be compared more than the behaviours of the individual logics. Because of this, we
have a single, general comparison interpretation applicable to all fuzzy logics, as it is
connected to their shared domain rather than different interpretations of implication.
4.2 Three axes of study for differentiable logics
We divide the properties proven in this work into three groups: algebraic, analytic,
and proof-theoretic.
(I) Algebraic properties
Interpretation via residuated lattices.
Definition 4.1. We say that a differentiable logic is interpretable in a distributive
residuated lattice if its operations satisfy axioms R1–10 of Table 2.
In addition, we say that
• a differentiable logic has negation defined in terms of implication if the operations
defined for it in Table 5 satisfy axiom N1 (Table 3);
• a differentiable logic has involutive negation if axioms axioms N2–N4 (Table 3);
• a differentiable logic has monoidal dual if axioms M1–M3 are satisfied (Table 4).
All of the chosen fuzzy logics are known to satisfy R1–10 and N1–N4, and some
of them have a monoidal dual. Our extension of DL2 satisfies only R1–10. STLν
operations do not satisfy either lattice or monoidal axioms. However, we will show that
STL∞does satisfy R1–R10 and N2–N4, signifying that at the limit, STL’s operators do
have desirable algebraic properties. Alas, they lose their desirable analytic properties
at the limit! This shows that the parameter ν of STLν may in fact serve as a switch
between the discrete and continuous worlds.
Idempotence.

the mutual absorption axioms (R5–R6) which are part of the axiomatisation of a
residuated lattice (Table 2), hence we do not consider it separately. Take ∨as an

(II) Analytic properties
Varnai and Dimarogonas [47] introduce three properties in this category: weak smoothness, scale-invariance, and shadow-lifting. The latter is the most important as it
accounts for gradual improvement in training. We only consider shadow-lifting here
as it is the most complex of those properties and leave the remaining properties to
future work.

Notice that classical conjunction does not satisfy the property of shadow-lifting: no

When one has to make a choice of a differentiable logic, different pragmatic considerations may influence that choice. If shadow-lifting is strictly desirable, then G¨odel,
 Lukasiewicz, and Yagerr logics are probably less desirable than the rest. However, further choice between logical properties is less clear. For example, one can imagine a
scenario when the specification language avoids negation, and in a style of substructural logics, treats differently p and p⊙p and thus sacrifices idempotence; in this case,
DL2 may provide an ideal translation function.
Conceptually, if one wishes to reconcile the three traditions: the algebraic theory of
substructural logics via residuated lattices, the analytic properties needed for machine
learning, and the proof-theoretic semantics via the Gentzen sequent calculus, then one
is looking, as a minimal requirement, to satisfy the properties listed in the first and the
final two columns of Table 6. In that case, two logics, product and DL2, bear promise.
Parametrised logics. An alternative approach to reconciling the properties of
Table 6 is to make a greater use of logics whose connectives are parametrised by a real
number as in STLν and Yagerr.
For example, taken together, STLν and STL∞, cover all properties in Table 6: if
ν ∈[1, ∞[, STLν satisfies shadow-lifting; for ν →∞, STLν convergences to STL∞, and
hence satisfies the algebraic theory of residuated lattices. Similarly, for r →∞, Yagerr
converges to G¨odel, with Yagerr satisfying involutive negation and G¨odel satisfying
idempotence.
Discrepancies in pen and paper proofs
Note that the formalisation further revealed some errors as explained below.
15

While doing the Rocq formalisation, we found two sources of errors in our pen and
paper proofs [49] as well as in the original STLν paper [47]. Firstly, the work in [49]
concerned completing results of Table 6 in the uniform notation of differentiable logics
(Fig. 1).
Some errors came from extension of fuzzy logics with comparison operators. The
interpretation of comparisons needed to be scaled between 0 and 1, and it seemed
obvious that the scaling was done correctly. Therefore we did not provide any proofs
concerning the interval properties of these operations in previous work, leading to
errors found and fixed in the course of the formalisation.
While a sketch of the shadow-lifting proof was provided in the original STLν
paper [47], it was incomplete and did not mention either the existence of other cases
as well as the reliance on L’Hˆopital’s rule.
5 A generic encoding of differentiable logics in Rocq
and its instantiations
In this section we address the aim of creating a generic and extendable encoding for
different differentiable logics, using uniform syntactic conventions. We highlight the
features of the encoding and the benefits of the general framework in proving properties
of similar differentiable logics.
5.1 A generic encoding of the syntax of types and expressions
The generic encoding of the types is the matter of declaring an inductive type corresponding to the syntax of Fig. 1. However, as different differentiable logics are defined
over different fragments of syntax, we need to account for this in the formalisation. We
do so by refining the Boolean type with flags that allow control over which fragments
of the syntax have defined semantics:
Inductive flag_neg := neg_def | neg_undef.
Inductive flag_impl := impl_def | impl_undef.
Inductive flag_monoid := m_def | m_undef.
Inductive flag_lattice := l_def | l_undef.
The individual flags control, respectively, whether negation ¬, implication ⇒,
monoidal operations ⊙and ⊕, and lattice operations ∧and ∨are defined. This
allows the inclusion of differentiable logics for which not all connectives have defined
semantics, such as DL2. Using the above flags we encode the syntax of the types of
differentiable logics (Fig. 1) as follows:
Inductive dl_type :=
| boolT of flag_neg & flag_impl & flag_monoid & flag_lattice
| indexT of nat | realT | vectorT of nat
| funT of nat & nat | fun2T of nat & nat & nat.
16

Following the semantics from Table 5 we define the types of the various differentiable
logics by selecting the right sets of flags, with all fuzzy differentiable logics grouped
together, for ease of reading:
Definition boolT_fuzzy := boolT neg_def impl_def m_def l_def.
Definition boolT_dl2 := boolT neg_undef impl_def m_def l_undef.
Definition boolT_stl := boolT neg_def impl_undef m_undef l_def.
As for differentiable logics’ expressions, their encoding is displayed in Fig. 2. It is
an inductive type indexed by dl_type so that the resulting syntax is intrinsically typed:
one cannot write ill-typed expressions. The Rocq inductive type matches the syntax
already explained in Fig. 1. Real expressions (line 7) use a type R of type realType coming from MathComp-Analysis [1] that represents real numbers. Boolean expressions
(line 5) use the native Rocq type bool. Indices embed an ordinal from MathComp
(line 6). More specifically, 'I_n is the type of natural numbers smaller than n. Last,
vectors embed finite functions of type 'I_n -> R, which in MathComp have a special
notation R ^ n (line 8). We name the lattice connectives ∧and ∨as dl_and and dl_or,
and include both the monoidal ⊙as dl_mand and ⊕as dl_mor. We encode those connectives as functions from the finite domain 'I_n into expressions to allow the inclusion
of STL2.
To ease reading, we will use notation such as a `/\ b or a `** b to denote binary
connectives (lattice and monoidal conjunction respectively) from now on. For a generic
definition of the syntax, we need to allow for the case of differentiable logics in which
negation is not defined (here: DL2). The explicit flags, such as neg_def for dl_not (line
14), signify that this flag is necessary for this connective to be defined. The constructor
dl_cmp (line 24) is for binary comparison operators over the real numbers; hereafter,
we will use notations such as `<= for the comparison corresponding to ≤instead of
“dl_cmp cmp_le” to ease reading. As for the last constructors, they are respectively for
functions, their application, both for one- and two-argument versions (dl_fun, dl_fun2
respectively for functions, with the same naming convention used for their application)
as per Fig. 1.

Proving these logical properties in Rocq consists of showing that they hold for the

numbers. As a consequence, its proof is a one-liner:
Lemma dl2_mandA f1 f2 (e1 e2 e3 : expr (boolT_def f1 m_def f2)) :
[[ e1 `** (e2 `** e3) ]]_dl2 = [[ (e1 `** e2) `** e3 ]]_dl2.

by rewrite /= !big_ord_recl /= !big_ord_recl !big_ord0 !addr0 addrA (*assoc. of
real number*) .
Qed.
Proofs for DL2 are similarly succinct.
In contrast, the proofs for Yagerr logic and STLν are more demanding. For
example, the proof of associativity for Yagerr:

arithmetics, simplfying the formalisation significantly.

In the following sections, we focus on shadow-lifting. We formally define shadowlifting in Sect. 7.2 and prove it for DL2 and product (which trivially enjoy shadowlifting) in Sect. 7.3. For STLν, this result was first proven (via a pencil-and-paper

and Yagerr fail shadow-lifting as they are not differentiable everywhere, due to their

7.2 Formalisation of shadow-lifting
As seen in Sect. 4.2, shadow-lifting is defined in terms of partial derivatives, for which
there was however no theory yet in MathComp-Analysis. They can be easily defined
on the model of derivatives [1, derive.v]. First, we define error vectors as row vectors
(type 'rV[R]_k where R is some ring and k the size of the vector) that are 0 everywhere
except at one coordinate i:
Definition err_vec {R : ringType} (i : 'I_n.+1) : 'rV[R]_n.+1 :=
\row_(j < n.+1) (i == j)%:R.
The notation %:R injects a natural number into a ring; note that here the Boolean
equality (notation ==) is implicitly coerced to a natural number. Then, given a function f that takes as input a row vector, we define a function partial that given a row
vector a and an index i returns the limit limh→0

Definition partial {R} {n} (f : 'rV[R]_n.+1 -> R) (a : 'rV[R]_n.+1) i :=
lim (h^-1 * (f (a + h *: err_vec i) - f a) @[h --> 0^']).
In this syntax, 0^' represents the deleted neighbourhood of 0, and lim g @ F represents
the limit of the function g at the filter F [5]. The notation *: represents scaling but
is equivalent to the multiplication of real numbers here. Hereafter, we use the Rocq
notation d f '/d i (reminiscent of
∂f
∂xi ) for partial f i.
Using partial derivatives, the definition of shadow-lifting (Definition 4.2) translates
directly into Rocq:
Definition shadow_lifting {R : realType} n (f : 'rV_n.+1 -> R) :=
forall p, p > 0 -> forall i, ('d f '/d i) (const_mx p) > 0.
The const_mx function comes from MathComp’s matrix theory and represents a
matrix where all coefficients are the given constant; we use it to implement the
restriction “pj = p” seen in Definition 4.2.

proof-theoretic properties of differentiable logics.
The main property we investigate is soundness (defined shortly in subsequent
sections). The soundness of fuzzy logics was proven by Metcalfe et al. [38, 39]. This
section provides the first formalisation of this result and extends the existing calculi
of fuzzy logics with derivable rules for the remaining connectives in comparison to the
minimal fragments presented in Figures 5–7. Last, this section presents new calculi
along with soundness and weak completeness proofs for DL2 as well as STL∞, neither
of which has previously appeared in the literature.
8.1 Hypersequent calculi for fuzzy logics
Real-valued logics, and in particular fuzzy logics, have been studied proof-theoretically
for decades. The full exposition of their proof-theoretic significance is beyond the scope
29

of this paper for which we refer the interested reader to the existing comprehensive
texts [29,39].
Generally, these logics are shown to be more expressive than intuitionistic logic,
but less expressive than classical logic, and for this reason they are sometimes called
superintuitionistic logics [29]. Both Hilbert-style and Gentzen-style calculi for fuzzy
logics exist. We only consider Gentzen-style (or sequent) calculi here or, more precisely,
we consider hypersequent calculi, as they have been established as the most standard
way to define fuzzy logics [39, Chapt. VI]. The calculi presented in this work follow
exactly those established in literature—which results in a single conclusion calculus
for G¨odel and multiple conclusion calculi for both Lukasiewicz and product.
Finally, recall that the connectives ⊙and ⇒correspond to the multiplicative fragment of linear logic, and that the connectives ∧, ∨, and ¬ correspond to the additive
fragment of linear logic. Thus, absence of some of the structural rules should not come
as a surprise; this has been studied as well in, e.g., [29,39].
Definition 8.1 (Sequent, Hypersequent [39]). A sequent is an ordered pair of finite
lists of formulas, written q1, . . . , qn ⊢p1, . . . , pm.
A hypersequent is a finite list of sequents of the form
Q1 ⊢P1| . . . | Qn ⊢Pn
where, for all i ∈{1, . . . , n}, Qi ⊢Pi is a sequent.
Figures 5–7 present three known sequent calculi covering the minimal fragments of
the G¨odel, product, and Lukasiewicz logics. As is standard in formalisation of sequent
calculi, we define each of these calculi as an inductive type. For example, Fig. 8 shows
the Rocq definition of the Lukasiewicz logic defined in Fig. 6.
The distinct features of these fuzzy logic calculi come from their unusual structural
rules. Firstly, handling the hypersequents necessitates introduction of structural rules
for manipulation of hypersequents, the so-called external structural rules (EW), (EC),
and (EEX) for hypersequent weakening, contraction, and exchange, respectively. Note
that internal structural rules differ across different calculi: the G¨odel logic has both
internal weakening and contraction, while the product and Lukasiewicz only have
weakening. All these logics have the internal exchange rule.
The structural rules called communication (COM), split (SPLIT), and mix (MIX)
are introduced for the purposes of completeness, as they enable proofs of some formulas that are of special interest for real-valued logics. For example, the pre-linearity
property
(p0 ⇒p1) ∨(p1 ⇒p0)
is supposed to reflect the fact that the real line forms a total order, in which for any two
given elements, one can prove that one of them is greater than the other. In absence
of internal weakening or contraction, this property usually requires some combination
of the structural rules (COM), (SPLIT), and (MIX), depending on the choice of the
fuzzy logic.

Formalisation.
When formalising in Rocq, we encode sequents as pairs of lists of formulas (using the
polymorphic lists of type seq) and hypersequents as lists of sequents. For readability
we use Q |- P to denote entailment. Each calculus is encoded as a separate inductive
type. In the interest of conciseness we use a short-term formula for the type of logical
formulas (e.g., formula := @expr R boolT_fuzzy for fuzzy differentiable logics) as well
as hypersequent := seq (seq formula * seq formula) for hypersequents.
Note that we initially considered defining sequents and hypersequents in terms of
multisets, as this would have allowed us to omit exchange rules. However, due to the
nesting of the structures needed in hypersequents and the richer library support for
polymorphic lists, lists tend to result in shorter proofs.

Recall that Q is a list of pairs; we use .1 and .2 to access the first and second element
of the pair respectively. We use exists2, a version of the existential quantifier that
asserts the existence a variable that satisfies two separate conditions.
The proofs are highly dependent on the semantics of the differentiable logic, especially the lattice or monoidal connectives used in the soundness definition. At the most
basic level, most of the cases can be reduced to a set of inequalities—therefore, while
they require significant simplification of the assumptions and proofs of intermediate
steps, we can make extensive use of the lra and nra tactics, decision procedures for
linear and non-linear arithmetic respectively [44]. Those simplifications include intermediate lemmas but also removal of unnecessary knowledge from the context and
occasional renaming of complex formulas (as both of those can cause said tactics to
fail). In the case of the product logic especially it is often necessary to prove more of
the intermediate steps manually, as they can be too complex for the nra tactic.
The proofs for G¨odel logic rely more on clever lemma application order (the
presence of minr/maxr necessitates either defining helper lemmas or unfolding each
34

occurrence). Finding the pattern of proof common among general cases significantly limits code duplication. In some cases, many nested occurrences of minr/maxr
can increase computation time needed to process the combination. Therefore it is
preferable to both make full use of targeted unfolding of only specific occurrences
(rewrite {n}/minr where n is the number of the occurrence to unfold) and develop
additional lemmas where possible—especially lemmas on the interactions between
minr, maxr and big operations.
Lastly, the majority of the cases can be proven directly, with the exception of the
rules for conjunction for G¨odel logic, which have to be proven by contradiction instead.
Extension of the calculi to differentiable logic syntax with derivable rules
The calculi presented for the fuzzy differentiable logics so far were for minimal subsets
of the language (Figures 5–7), not the entirety of the differentiable logic syntax. We,
in fact, prove soundness both of those standardly used calculi, as well as the calculi for
the full set of connectives used for differentiable logics (following the syntax of Fig. 1),
with the exception of ⊕for product logic, because since it is not definable in terms of
other connectives its rule cannot be derived.
We do not formalise completeness of the extended calculi in this work, but we
compensate by showing that the rules in the extended calculi that include the full set
of connectives can be derived from the calculi for the minimal fragments (Figures 5–7)
which are known to be complete.
As an interesting observation, the derivability sometimes requires an addition of
extended (yet still derivable) logical rules to the calculus. For example, to derive
the sequent rules for monoidal conjunction for Lukasiewicz logic, an “extended” left
implication rule, (L ⇒)e, is necessary:
G | Q ⊢P | G | Q, p1 ⊢p0, P

This rule can be derived using the standard implication rule (L ⇒), weakening (W)
and external exchange (EC). We note it here explicitly as the use of such alternate
rules in derivation is often not stated in the original paper proofs [39].
8.3 Hypersequent calculus for DL2
The main challenge of designing the calculus for DL2 [27] lies in making the adjustments to its original definition and fitting it into the residuated lattice framework
(Table 5). Recall that its original proposed interpretation of ⊕(multiplication) is not
a dual to the interpretation of ⊙and furthermore, that due to lack of the structural
negation (as it is interpreted at the level of comparisons between reals) there is no
simple way to obtain a dual (Sect. 6). Therefore, it is not included in the calculus.
Secondly, its implication was also only defined in terms of negation, and therefore was
similarly non-structural and only expressible with a combination of logical connectives and comparisons between real numbers instead of being a separate connective.
In order to include it within the calculus, a new implication (Table 5) satisfying the
residuation rule was added.

From the perspective of formalisation in Rocq this proof has posed a different
type of technical challenge. While, mathematically, the proof is very similar to the
one of G¨odel logic, in the majority of the cases, it instead uses \bar R (the type
of extended real numbers in MathComp-Analysis) as its type. This is due to the
explicit inclusion of −∞and ∞in the semantics of STL∞, which was necessary for
proving it is a residuated lattice (Sect. 6): specifically ∞was needed as an identity
element for its monoidal operator ⊙. Working with extended reals can provide an
additional challenge due to a smaller library of available lemmas. In this particular
case it also leads to the inability of using lra and nra tactics [44], which the G¨odel
formalisation relies heavily on, and instead solving every inequality set manually.
We do not propose a sequent calculus for full STLν, as we assume that, due to
the lack of associativity of this DL, both algebraic and proof-theoretic semantics will
necessitate approaches that are rather different to the ones we considered in this paper.
For future work, one could try to take inspiration from the proof theory for nonassociative logics, such as non-associative Lambek calculus [34,40], when studying the
full STLν.

8.5 Weak completeness
Recall the axiom schema arising from residuated lattices (Table 2), which we established for the semantics of differentiable logics in Sect. 6. In the absence of a full
completeness result, we can employ a collection of axioms to demonstrate weak completeness for the calculi. By ensuring that these axioms are provable within the target
calculus—rather than treating them as properties of the semantics—we guarantee that
desirable properties are preserved by the calculus. This approach can provide reasonable confidence that the system is well-behaved. Most importantly if one of the axioms
is not provable, thus the calculus is not weakly complete, it gives sufficient evidence
against completeness of that given sequent calculi. This is therefore especially important for the calculi of DL2 and STL∞, for which we do not provide a completeness
proof.
Definition 8.6 (Weak Completeness). A hypersequent calculus is weakly complete
if relevant axioms of R1–R9 are provable.
We omit specifically axiom R10, as residuation is a semantic property. We determine relevant axioms based on the properties of the differentiable logic in question
(Table 6).
By using the derivation within the respective sequent calculi we show that DL2 [4,
seq_calc_dl2.v] and STL∞[4, seq_calc_stli.v] are weakly complete.
We leave the problem of finding complete sequent calculus rules for negation of
STL∞for future work.
9 Example of applications in machine learning
We return to the motivation behind differentiable logics, property-guided training for
verification. For this, we use as an example ϵ-δ-robustness, a property often used in
neural network verification [18].
9.1 Property-guided training, by means of an example
Neural network properties
Given a neural network N : Rm →Rn, the a property to be verified usually takes the
form of a Hoare triple ∀x ∈Rm.P(x) −→S(x), where P and S can be arbitrary properties obtained by using variables x ∈Rm, constants, vector, arithmetic operations,
≤, =, ∧, ∨, and ¬. Additionally, S may contain the neural network N as a function.
Example 9.1 (ϵ-δ-robustness [18]). Given a neural network N and a vector v, consider the specification that requires that for all inputs x that are within an ϵ distance
from v, the output of N(x) should not deviate by more than δ from N(v):
∀x.|x −v|L∞≤ϵ ⇒|N(x) −N(v)|L∞≤δ.
40

This property can be used to avoid misclassifying images when only a few pixels are perturbed. This particular example uses the L∞norm: |x −y|L∞
def
=
maxi∈{0,...,n−1}([x]i −[y]i), where [x]i stands for the ith element of x.
Unfortunately, as demonstrated by Fischer et al. [27], even most accurate neural
networks fail even the most natural verification properties, such as ϵ-δ-robustness.
This motivated the search for better ways to train the networks.
Property-guided training
Methods for property-guided training have received considerable attention in the AI
literature, as the survey [31] shows. We will only illustrate the method that was
suggested by Fischer et al. [27], and refer the reader to the survey for more examples.
Example 9.2 (Generating a loss function from a logical property [27]). Recall that
standard supervised learning trains a neural network N with trainable parameters θ to
optimise the objective minθ L(x, y), for the loss function L : Rm ×Rn →R. Generally,
L measures the difference between the network’s output and the given data for each
input point. Examples of L are cross-entropy loss or mean squared error. But now we
want to train the neural network to satisfy any arbitrary property ∀x.P(x) −→S(x).
For this, we replace the above optimisation objective with
min
θ

max
x∈HP(x) LS(x)

where HP(x) ⊆Rm refines the type Rm to a subset for which the property P holds,
and LS : Rm →R is obtained by applying a suitable interpretation function for S.
We omit the exact details of how such optimisation algorithms are defined: they
are known and can be found in a suitable machine learning tutorial, for example [33].
Intuitively, the optimisation algorithm will search for x ∈HP(x) such that x maximises
the loss LS(x), in order to train the neural network parameters θ to minimise that loss.
Concretely, if the property is ϵ-δ-robustness, it will look for the worst perturbation of
v that violates the property, and will optimise the neural network to classify that bad
example correctly.
Differentiable logics for loss functions
In the above example, we did not explain how to define the interpretation function
LS for an arbitrary property S; we need differentiable logics for that purpose.
Example 9.3 (Loss functions from properties in a fuzzy logic). Taking the property
from Example 9.1, by the Fischer et al. method we must be able to interpret the righthand side of the implication, i.e., |N(x) −N(v)|L∞≤δ, given concrete values for ϵ, δ,
a concrete vector v, neural network N, and a suitable definition of the L∞norm. For
example, interpretation for our property in STLν [47] is given by J|N(x)−N(v)|L∞≤

10 Related work
Neural network verification is a new field, and its nascent methods need validation
and further refinement. Better understanding, formalisation and implementation of
differentiable logics is often named as one of the main challenges in the field [21].
42

In terms of programming language support for neural network verification, the
CAISAR tool [30], implemented as an OCaml DSL, puts emphasis on the smooth integration of a general specification language with many existing neural network solvers.
However, CAISAR does not support property-guided training. The aspiration of languages like Vehicle [24] and CAISAR is to accommodate compilation of specifications
into both neural network solver and machine learning backends. For the former, there
is an on-going work on certifying the neural network solver backends [23–25].
On the side of machine-learning backends, differentiable logics have been previously formalised in the Agda interactive theorem prover as part of the formalisation of
the Vehicle language [9], but did not extend to shadow-lifting—the part that requires
extensive mathematical libraries. Property-guided training certified via theorem
proving was also proposed by Chevallier et al. [19].
Relevant work on formalisation of neural networks in ITPs includes: verification
of neural networks in Isabelle/HOL [16] and Imandra [25], formalisation of piecewise
affine activation functions in Rocq [8], providing formal guarantees of the degree to
which the trained neural network will generalise to new data in Rocq [14], convergence
of a single-layered perceptron in Rocq [41], and verification of neural archetypes
in Rocq [36]. The formalisation presented here does not directly formalise neural
networks.
Prompted by the increasing demand from AI, there has been a surge of research into
mathematical foundations of quantitative logics, of which differentiable logics can be
seen as a special case. Firstly, Bacci et al. [10,11] formalised a logic of Lawvere quantale,
over the domain [0, ∞]. The logic bears some resemblance to DL2 thanks to its domain,
and also the authors have shown that the Lukasiewicz logic is an instance of the logics
of Lawvere quantale. The structure of Lawvere Quantale generalises the definition
of residuated lattice, and also this logic has a proof-theoretic interpretation [10, 11].
Bacci and M¨ogelberg [12] further extended that logic with the induction principle,
and showed how it can be applied to encode Probabilistic Hoare logic and Markov
Processes.
At the same time, Capucci [17] presented a logic that promises even a greater generality. It also takes a Lawvere quantale as its interpretation, thus retaining the link
with the tradition of residuated lattices. But at the same time it defines multiplicative
and additive connectives that yield a sequent calculus similar to that of the Linear
logic. Notably, its monoidal operators resemble those of the product logic we have
considered here. For the lattice/additive component, that logic offers to use soft additive connectives ∧and ∨. These connectives have a softness parameter, that, at ∞,
turns them into min and max; while at all other values retains the shadow-lifting and
smoothness properties. This resembles a lot the STLν logic intuition, with its use of
parameter ν. Capucci’s logic is under development, yet it promises to bring a conceptually elegant resolution to the quest for harmonious co-existence of algebraic, analytic
and proof-theoretic properties in one logic. We are looking into formalisation of that
logic in Rocq [37].
Different variants of the logics we use in this work—fuzzy logics in particular—
can also be considered as alternative differentiable logics. The primary example being
the choice of type of implication [45]. The implication required by the properties of
43

residuated lattices and used through this paper are R-implications, but S-implications
as well as Yager f- and g-implications are other well-known classes with different
semantic properties [13]. We have investigated some of the alternate semantics, in
particular involving S-implications and S-negations, residuation does not always hold.
On the other hand, the extension of differentiable logics, particularly fuzzy logics, with
comparisons between real numbers is reminiscent of SMT [15], as both involve adding
domain-specific theories on top of propositional logic.
A property found desirable for differentiable logics which we do not consider in
this work is adequacy relative to Boolean logic (in ML works sometimes referred to as
“soundness” [27]). Proving a logic to be adequate shows it to be weaker than classical
logic—a property commonly seen within the field of substructural logics which are
shown to be stronger than intuitionistic logic and weaker than classical [29]. While
adequacy of fuzzy logics is well known, the introduction of comparisons between real
numbers has a non-trivial effect on the result and is left as future work.
Lastly, we would be remiss not to mention the developments in the application
of differentiable logics in machine learning. In the literature, fuzzy logics have been
introduced alongside comparative analyses of their performance in training tasks [45].
Krieken et al. [46] have also loosely included them in a broader scene of neurosymbolic methods in terms of their assumptions of independence of different concepts.
More recently, Flinkow et al. [28] conducted a comprehensive evaluation of various
differentiable logics—including all logics considered in the present work except STLν—
employing a neural network verifier; although the differentiable logics’ semantics differ
slightly, their evaluation provides information about the performance of differentiable
logics in training.
11 Conclusions and future work
We have presented a complete Rocq formalisation of a range of existing differentiable
logics, making the following three main contributions:
1. We provided a uniform formalisation of algebraic, analytic, and proof-theoretic
results for differentiable logics that span a wide range of substructural logic and
machine learning papers, published by different communities in the last 20 years. We
showed that unification of these three aspects is crucial for adequate understanding
and future design of logics for machine learning. Moreover, we showed that such a
unifying approach is possible.
2. We contribute to the differentiable logic community by revisiting semantics of
STLν and DL2 in a way more amenable to formal verification. We found and fixed
errors in the literature. Indeed, our previous attempt [49] to do this with pen and
paper proofs was drowned in low-level case analysis and resulted in some errors,
see Section 4.3. This complexity was our initial motivation to undertake the formalisation. We moreover presented a new sequent calculus for DL2 that could be
derived thanks to our general unifying approach to differentiable logics.
3. We proposed a general formalisation strategy to differentiable logics based on
dependent types and formal mathematics. The proposed formalisation is built to
be easily extendable for future studies of different differentiable logics.

Table 7 summarises the Rocq implementation. Both L’Hˆopital’s rule (now a part
of MathComp-Analysis [1, realfun.v] [3]) and analytic properties, especially in
complex cases such as STLν, form a substantial part of the development. The proofs for
fuzzy differentiable logics (file fuzzy.v) are grouped together thanks to their similarity
and even share some of the proofs. The files mathcomp_extra.v and analysis_extra.v
contain utility lemmas for, respectively, the MathComp and MathComp-Analysis
libraries. The file mathcomp_extra.v has a selection of lemmas on iterated operators
(e.g., iterated sums, n-ary maximum), including lemmas for said operations when
restricted to the domain [0, 1] used by fuzzy logics. The file analysis_extra.v, on the
other hand, contains multiple lemmas on properties of min and max for extended reals.
During our work, we completed missing parts of the shadow-lifting proof for STLν; for
example, the original STLν proof failed to acknowledge the need for L’Hˆopital’s rule.
Regarding the concrete formalisation strategy, it was revealing that most of our
formalisation was coherent with the standard MathComp libraries (and standard
mathematical results), and the library extensions we needed were natural (e.g.,
L’Hˆopital’s rule). This work hence demonstrates that Rocq and MathComp are
effective working tools to formalise state-of-the-art AI results: DL2 and STLν were
published in recent conferences [27,47] and this paper formalises the most significant
results from both.
In the end, we have a uniform formalisation where all differentiable logics are
“tamed” which provides solid ground for formalisation of methods deployed in
verification of neural networks.
45

Future work
We plan to consider other definitions of soundness, and other differentiable logics,
including STLν with revised negation. We conjecture that Definition 4.2 allows for
generalisation (removing the condition “pj = p”) but this is left for future work. The
trade-off between idempotence, associativity, and shadow-lifting that was conjectured
by Varnai and Dimarogonas [47] is reminiscent of substructural logics and suggests
investigating the connection. Establishing connection of this work with the logics of
Lawvere quantale by Bacci et al. [10] or by Capucci [17] might also provide new tools
to study differentiable logics.
Additionally, we have several possible improvements to the formalisation itself in
mind. Firstly, we could generalise our formalisation by introducing a structure for
residuated lattices instead of specialising to real numbers. Furthermore, we believe
that the use of multisets [20] instead of polymorphic lists (the seq type in MathComp)
in the implementation of the hypersequent calculi would ultimately provide better
support when coupled with appropriate automation. Namely, due to lack of exchange
rules in calculi modelled on multisets, automation of the proofs could be more modular;
this would however require substantial effort to match the level of support enjoyed
with polymorphic lists.
We leave the formalisation of completeness of the calculi, as well as result related
to cut-free proofs [39], for future work. We also suggest as a simpler but weaker
alternative a notion of weak completeness—ensuring that axioms from Table 2 are
provable within the target calculus. This, while it does not guarantee completeness,
can be seen as a pre-requisite to it, as it would standardly be proven first before
algebraically proving how this set of axioms is complete relative to real numbers. This
provides some intuition as tho why Yagerr logic may not have a calculus—it is easy
to see that in it, prelinearity is not satisfied. If we take Jp0KY = Jp1KY = 0.5 and r = 2

Separately from the questions of scientific curiosity and mathematical elegance,
there is a question of lacking programming language support for machine learning.
As tools like Vehicle [24] and CAISAR [30] are being proposed to provide a more
principled approach to verification of machine learning, in the long term, compilers of
these new emerging languages will require certification. And this, in turn, will demand
formalisation of results such as the ones we presented here. The formalisation of differentiable logics would hence directly contribute to certified compilation of specification
languages to machine learning libraries.
Finally, first-order quantification for quantitative logics generally and differentiable
logics in particular, has been a long-standing open problem, discussed in our work [49]
46

and the work of others [12,17]. Thanks to accumulated knowledge of these logics, we
stand very close to its resolution.
